channel.title: Comments for page "All Things Considered"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "All Things Considered"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, no, you're right, and this is supposed to be essentially Baseline Earth, so I'll fix that. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A little nitpick, but the University of California isn't a university, because it refers to the state's public university system. There are UC Los Angeles, UC Riverside, etc. unless this is part of the alternate reality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Or we prepared a vaccine for them to use, but in order for it to be useful we had to teach them what a vaccine is and how it works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If nothing else, in the original 1322 the Foundation designed a vaccine for them (without firsthand detailed knowledge of their biology) whereas in this one we apparently taught them about vaccines and they applied it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It really makes me wonder. If the vaccine does eventually cause sterility in this version of events, how much coverage will that disaster get?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll do you one better. I worked for WOSU as an intern. My mother in law works for OSU development (read: donations.).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We know how this ended the first time. It was such a sad story.

And now that we've fleshed it out a bit more, it's even sadder. : (

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hope the vaccine program works better this time around…

Also, +1. The very first article I've read that makes me question whether or not the Foundation is a good thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hope that's a good headdesk…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the two scientists touch hands.

/headdesk

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It makes me so sad to know how this is all going to go downhill…

Though, since different (and probably, many more) scientists worked on the vaccine in this canonality, the vaccine won't sterilize them.

Maybe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was chilled when I realized which SCP this was. Marvelous work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The world hasn't ended, the people are living with and accepting things, and people are being people. This seems pretty much exactly how a foundationless world with these anomolies would act. Rationalization and acceptance. Also, this is an amazing peice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is beautiful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This sounds exactly how NPR would cover this kind of story. Good work, and +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: High praise. Thank you, Spike. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am not sure how to feel about that threat. XD

Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Of course, without the Foundation, who's to say that the same thing will even happen? The line about the vaccine suggests that it will, but there's a chance, however small, that if the people on the other side hadn't relatively quickly declared war, that the infertility problem could have been reversed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Photo, you got the voice/tone of All Things Considered down perfectly. I'm half tempted to send them a link to this… ;-)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Brilliantly written. Well done, Photosynthetic. All my +1.

The author and I carried on a little bit of correspondence before she drafted this. I'll just post some of my half of the exchange:
—-
… When I wrote SCP-1322, I intentionally omitted most of the details about the Hartle culture. Part of this was in order to make it a short story rather than a longer world-building exercise, but another part of it was in order to implicitly get across the fact that, fundamentally, the Foundation really isn't all that interested in the Hartle culture. They're more interested in the anomaly itself— in whether it constitutes a threat that needs to be secured and contained.

So you could pretty much make up whatever you wanted about the Hartle culture. My preference, however, is that if you do so, don't make broad-brush statements (like "the reason they're not interested in hearing about our medicine or religion is that they're deeply devoted to their own religion, which has Christian Science-like notions about healing"). Even though your story would not really be canonical, plenty of people are going to assume that your Hartle is the same as the one in my head…

—-

One more thing I'll say— I think it's important to stress that the SCP-1322-1 civilization is, basically, regular people. Like us. They're not incomprehensibly foreign. This is part of what makes the tragedy of my original piece work. A piece about sterilizing a planet inhabited by inscrutable hive-mind lobster people or whatever would be a shame, but not necessarily a tragedy which is why I didn't write it that way. Instead, these folks have families. They write poetry. They go on vacation and sit on the beach and watch the sun set.

The other half of the tragedy is that this whole society is slowly but inexorably dying, but the Foundation doesn't give a fuck. The bastards.

—-

(TL;DR: The SCP-1322 in this piece is a bit different than the one in my head-canon, but I love this piece. It makes the original 1322 even more tragic, because in my head-canon the Foundation's relationship with the other side was much, much more cold and detached. In my imagination, the Hartle folks are in a sense much more "human" than the Foundation people on "our" side.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"we've taught them about vaccines, and they're already halting a pandemic in its tracks"
Crap. Anyone who read the SCP will know the reason for my comment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A convincing replica of NPR. I like this look at the anomaly; it's always been one of my favorites.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I said oh, fuck at thie time:

it's awfully hard to really hurt one another through such a tiny aperture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Remember, in our canon, there IS no Foundation. NASA is handling the Hartle Anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why oh fuck? Because of the pandemic?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Srsly? :D I used to, before I moved away.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, WOSU?

I listen to the exact same station. o.o

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this.

Also: oh, fuck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …Huh. It is. Well, I mainlined the actual radio show for a week while trying to write this, so I guess I should've expected some unconscious influence. (Part of it may be that in my head, this host's first name is pronounced "uh-LEE-shuh".)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was just wondering, as the host's name is rather similar to one of the current hosts of NPR, Mellisa Block. But maybe it's just me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Should probably wait til the contest is over, but if you want to see it done, I am all over that. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nope, I just invented people. It's set in an alternate universe, after all, and it felt more than a little weird when I tried using actual NPR people the first time around.

And yes, Fleischer's narrating right there. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just asking for an audio adaptation…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I listen to NPR regularly. As a radio station, they narrate the events that happen if they're talking about a story live, as… well, you can't exactly watch radio, can you?

Also, were the people in the story meant to be actual NPR commentators? If so, I think you mispelled the name of the host…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
FLEISCHER: One of her assistants holds up a small metal tube.

Should this be in parentheses or brackets if it isn't an actual quote from Fleischer?

Eithre way, I love 1322 and this kicks ass. An upvote is you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, a slightly different version of SCP-1322 and the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Live coverage of SCP-1322? I like.
